body_txt: In the United States, the Fifth and Sixth Amendments to the Constitution provide valuable rights to those arrested or accused of a crime, be you innocent or guilty... provided you know they exist. A suspect may not be well verse in constitutional law, and remembering the rules is not easy when your hands are in cuffs and your face is being smashed against the trunk of a police cruiser. In 1966, the US Supreme Court decided the case of Miranda v. Arizona , in which they declared that arrestees were required to be informed of specific rights, primarily the right against self-incrimination and the right to legal representation. The court did not dictate an exact wording of the warning, so private companies began printing cards to sell to police departments with a pre-written "Miranda Warnings", to help ensure that the full rights are properly communicated. This led to the slang "read them their rights", which continues to the present day, long after memorization of the Miranda Warning became a standard of police training. The exact wording used varies, but it goes something like this:  In some states the following is added:  In fiction, the Miranda Rights note The rights themselves exist independent of the court's decision on Miranda - they are in the Constitution - and the warning is only meant to remind you that they exist, although any legal scholar will recognize that, say, the Fifth Amendment right against self-incrimination falls under the umbrella heading of "Miranda Rights" while the Third Amendment right to not have to quarter troops in your home does not. are frequent victims of Hollywood Law . Some common deviations from reality:      People aren't always Mirandized upon arrest either. The warning is only required before an interrogation, and so it may be given in an interrogation room, which ensures that the warning (and any waiving of rights) is recorded. The danger is that any questions asked before the "official" interrogation begins could be considered improper, and anything a suspect lets slip during that time might not be admissible. For these reasons, many departments have a policy of issuing the warning as soon as a suspect is arrested. Incidentally, if you are ever Mirandized, even if you are convinced you've done nothing wrong (in fact, especially if you are convinced you've done nothing wrong), the only words out of your mouth should be "I want a lawyer and will not consider answering questions until I have one." See here for 49 minutes of extremely enlightening and entertaining education on the topic note ... that said, also check out this podcast episode starting around 17 minutes in for a breakdown of all the things that video overstates, obfuscates or just plain gets wrong . Seriously, the line of thought which says " Only Bad Guys Call Their Lawyers " is wrong on more levels than we can count. The British version is (oddly enough) a little less formal: You Do Not Have to Say Anything . For anything not US or UK see Reading Your Rights . Subtrope of Artistic License – Law Enforcement .
